original_hypothesis.title: Gas-bounded checkpoint logic may permanently desync voting power after ~5.3 days inactivity (WEEK=1800), breaking totalSupply/vote accounting
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: VotingEscrow._checkpoint() advances global point history in a loop capped to 255 iterations, each step advancing by WEEK. With WEEK=1800 seconds, a single call can only catch up ~5.3 days. If the system goes longer without successful checkpointing, global history cannot be fully updated in one call, potentially causing persistent mis-accounting in totalSupplyAtT/totalSupplyAt and downstream reward distribution calculations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic, on-chain reachable correctness failure that can occur naturally (low activity) or be induced by grief (discourage checkpointing). It affects core invariants around total voting power and can impact emissions/rebases/claims that depend on historical supply.

inventory_refs.function: totalSupplyAtT(uint t, uint epoch, mapping(uint => int128) storage slope_changes, Data storage VotingBalanceLogicData)

proof_of_signal.pattern: _checkpoint() for-loop `for (uint i = 0; i < 255; ++i)` advancing `t_i += WEEK` where WEEK = 1800 (BlackTimeLibrary)

preconditions: No successful checkpointing interaction (deposit/withdraw/merge/split/checkpoint) occurs for > 255*WEEK seconds
Protocol continues to rely on totalSupplyAtT/totalSupplyAt for rewards/governance
At least one state-changing operation later calls _checkpoint() expecting it to fully catch up

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: bounded historical catch-up loop with extremely short epoch size
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: point_history may stop being updated to current timestamp, making slope/bias schedule application incomplete
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no mechanism to iterate across multiple calls atomically or to enforce periodic checkpointing

analysis_questions: If gap exceeds 255*WEEK, does _checkpoint stop early leaving last_point.ts < block.timestamp?
Do subsequent calls eventually catch up by repeated interactions, or does the design require full catch-up in a single call to remain correct?
Which core operations (RewardsDistributor claim math, governance snapshots) become wrong or revert as a result?
validation_requirements: Simulate time jump >6 days and execute checkpoint; assert point_history[epoch].ts == block.timestamp (or expected)
Check totalSupplyAtT monotonicity and consistency pre/post gap
Assess downstream impact on RewardsDistributor calculations and voting

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Protocol experiences low activity for >6 days; the first user interaction afterward cannot fully update the global checkpoints due to the 255-iteration bound, leading to incorrect total supply and potentially misallocated/disrupted reward calculations for many users.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High systemic risk: mispriced supply affects reward distribution (rebases) and could distort governance and emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Potentially all veNFT holders and reward recipients
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require repeated calls, manual maintenance transactions, or contract upgrade; historical accuracy may be compromised

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing long-gap time travel tests given WEEK=1800

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor point_history[epoch].ts lag vs block.timestamp and mismatches in totalSupplyAtT

validation_result.mechanism: bounded checkpoint loop with short WEEK constant
validation_result.impact_summary: With WEEK=1800 and a 255-iteration cap, _checkpoint can only catch up ~5.31 days. Any inactivity beyond this permanently desyncs global point_history until multiple catch-up calls occur, causing incorrect totalSupply and reward miscalculations.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _checkpoint function in VotingEscrow.sol uses a for loop capped at 255 iterations, each advancing by WEEK (1800 seconds from BlackTimeLibrary). This allows a maximum catch-up of 255 * 1800 = 459,000 seconds ≈ 5.31 days. In the original Curve/Solidly design, WEEK was 604,800 seconds (7 days), allowing ~4.9 years of catch-up. With WEEK=1800, a mere 5.31 days of inactivity breaks the global point_history. After the loop exhausts its 255 iterations without reaching block.timestamp, the global point stored at point_history[epoch] has ts < block.timestamp. User modifications (slope/bias changes) are applied to this stale intermediate point. The _supply_at function in VotingBalanceLogic.sol has the same 255-iteration limit, compounding the issue: even if a stale point is found, forward iteration to the target time may also fail if the gap is too large. While repeated calls to checkpoint() can gradually close the gap, during the catch-up period all totalSupply calculations—used by RewardsDistributor for claim calculations and governance for quorum—return incorrect values.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: BlackTimeLibrary.sol line 6 confirms WEEK=1800. VotingEscrow.sol lines ~215-245 show the 255-iteration loop advancing by WEEK. When the loop exhausts without reaching block.timestamp, last_point.ts remains behind current time. VotingBalanceLogic.sol _supply_at (lines ~168-190) has the same 255-iteration WEEK-step loop. RewardsDistributor._claim (lines ~125-135) calls totalSupplyAtT which depends on accurate point_history. The code comment at line ~221 even acknowledges this: 'Hopefully it won't happen that this won't get used in 5 years!' — a comment from the original design where 255 weeks ≈ 4.9 years, now obsolete with WEEK=1800.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: With WEEK=1800, loop covers only 255*1800=459000 seconds (~5.31 days). If no checkpoint occurs for >5.31 days, the loop exhausts without reaching block.timestamp, leaving point_history[epoch].ts < block.timestamp. User slope/bias changes are then applied to a stale intermediate point.

Same 255-iteration WEEK-step limit. If the starting point is more than 5.31 days behind target time t, _supply_at cannot fully iterate to t, returning incorrect total supply that omits slope changes from skipped periods.

WEEK=1800 (30 minutes) is the root cause. The original Curve design used WEEK=604800 (7 days), where 255*WEEK≈4.9 years. With WEEK=1800, 255*WEEK≈5.31 days—a realistically achievable inactivity window.

calling_sequence: 2. _checkpoint reads epoch and point_history[epoch] to get last_point (ts from last checkpoint)
3. Loop: t_i = (last_checkpoint/WEEK)*WEEK, then for i=0..254: t_i += WEEK, apply slope_changes
4. If t_i never reaches block.timestamp in 255 iterations, loop exits normally (no break)
5. epoch = _epoch is stored; point_history[_epoch] is stored with last_point.ts < block.timestamp
6. Subsequently, totalSupplyAtT(block.timestamp) calls getPastGlobalPointIndex to find latest point
7. _supply_at starts from that stale point, also limited to 255 iterations, potentially failing to reach target time
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When block.timestamp - last_checkpoint_ts > 255*1800 (459000 seconds / ~5.31 days), the for loop in _checkpoint exhausts all 255 iterations advancing t_i by 1800 each time. Since t_i never equals block.timestamp, the break is never hit. After the loop, epoch is stored with point_history[epoch].ts = last_checkpoint_aligned + 255*1800 < block.timestamp. All subsequent totalSupplyAtT queries start from this stale point. The _supply_at function has the same 255-iteration limit, so the total forward reach from a single checkpoint call + supply query is 2*255*1800 = 918000 seconds (~10.6 days). Gaps exceeding this cause persistent supply miscalculation.

deployment_assumptions_checked: WEEK is a compile-time constant of 1800 in BlackTimeLibrary.sol
config_validation.deployment_evidence: BlackTimeLibrary.sol line 6: uint256 internal constant WEEK = 1800. This is hardcoded, not configurable at deployment. No deployment scripts modify this value.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is realistic: a protocol on a low-activity chain (or during a holiday/weekend period) may go 6+ days without any VotingEscrow interaction (deposit, withdraw, merge, split, or explicit checkpoint). With WEEK=1800, this exceeds the 5.31-day catch-up limit. The first interaction afterward cannot fully update point_history. Multiple checkpoint() calls are needed to close the gap, but between calls, totalSupply is incorrect. RewardsDistributor._claim uses totalSupplyAtT to compute reward shares (balance_of * tokens_per_week / supply), so incorrect supply directly causes incorrect reward distributions. An attacker could deliberately avoid checkpointing during low-activity periods, then exploit the stale totalSupply to claim disproportionate rewards.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH SEVERITY: (1) Global point_history desyncs from actual time, corrupting the core accounting invariant. (2) totalSupply() and totalSupplyAtT() return incorrect values, affecting RewardsDistributor claim calculations—users receive wrong reward amounts. (3) Governance voting via getPastTotalSupply returns wrong quorum values. (4) The issue compounds: each gap requires multiple manual checkpoint() calls to resolve, with no automatic mechanism. (5) With WEEK=1800, this can occur after just ~5.31 days of inactivity—a plausible scenario for any protocol.
recommended_mitigations: Increase the iteration cap significantly (e.g., to 1024 or higher) to accommodate WEEK=1800, or revert WEEK to a standard value like 604800
Add a multi-call checkpoint mechanism that allows catching up across multiple transactions atomically
Implement a keeper/cron bot that calls checkpoint() at least every 5 days (preferably every epoch)
Add a check after the loop that reverts if t_i != block.timestamp, forcing callers to recognize the gap and call checkpoint multiple times
Consider storing a 'stale' flag when the loop cannot catch up, preventing incorrect totalSupply queries until fully caught up
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VotingEscrow with WEEK=1800. 2. Create a lock and checkpoint. 3. Advance time by 7 days (604800 seconds > 459000). 4. Call checkpoint(). 5. Assert that point_history[epoch].ts < block.timestamp (proving incomplete catch-up). 6. Call totalSupplyAtT(block.timestamp) and verify it returns an incorrect value. 7. Call checkpoint() repeatedly until point_history[epoch].ts == block.timestamp, counting required calls. 8. Verify RewardsDistributor.claim returns wrong amounts between stale and caught-up states.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol has an off-chain keeper that reliably calls checkpoint() within the 5.31-day window (no on-chain evidence of such requirement)
Whether repeated checkpoint() calls eventually produce fully correct accounting or whether intermediate stale writes introduce permanent drift in slope/bias tracking
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with various gap sizes (5, 6, 10, 30 days) to quantify totalSupply error magnitude
Test RewardsDistributor claim amounts before and after catch-up to measure financial impact
Verify that the accumulated rounding errors from multiple partial catch-up calls do not cause permanent drift
Test interaction between _checkpoint gap and _supply_at gap to determine worst-case combined error
